findings.claim: The 'baseline secretary methods' and 'baseline prophet methods' adequately represent the best existing unfair approaches for comparison.
findings.failure_mechanism: The baselines may be suboptimal, outdated, or not specifically designed for the multi-color setting, making it easy for proposed methods to appear competitive even if they underperform against true SOTA unfair algorithms.
findings.evidence_test: Explicitly list and cite the baselines used, provide their theoretical performance guarantees in the multi-color setting, and include results from any other relevant unfair SOTA methods.

findings.claim: The prior probability p (P[best from group]) is known accurately a priori and remains stationary for the fair algorithms to achieve their fairness guarantees.
findings.failure_mechanism: Fair algorithms likely condition decisions on p (e.g., group-specific thresholds); estimation error, drift, or lack of advance knowledge would degrade fairness (P[select group] ≠ p) and utility simultaneously.
findings.evidence_test: Ablation experiments perturbing p (e.g., ±10-20% misspecification) and measuring resulting fairness violation (e.g., |P[select G] - true p|) and utility drop.

findings.claim: Raw average value of selected candidate is an appropriate performance metric for prophet algorithms across parameter settings.
findings.failure_mechanism: Parameter settings (e.g., number of colors, group sizes) alter value scales or E[max], so raw averages incomparably favor settings with higher absolute values rather than relative competitive performance.
findings.evidence_test: Report normalized competitive ratios (E[selected]/E[offline max]) alongside raw averages for all algorithms and settings.

findings.claim: Real-data experiments validly reflect the theoretical online sequential model assumptions (e.g., random permutation arrival, value independence conditional on group).
findings.failure_mechanism: Real data typically exhibits temporal correlations, non-random arrival orders biased by quality/group, or intra-group value dependencies beyond the prior p, causing algorithms tuned for IID/permutation models to fail.
findings.evidence_test: Describe real dataset(s), arrival order generation (e.g., shuffled?), tests for permutation-invariance (resample orders), group-value independence (conditional stats), and stationarity.

critic_r1_output.summary: The methodology has material gaps in baseline rigor, untested sensitivity to core prerequisite p, suboptimal prophet metric, and unverified real-data modeling assumptions, weakening empirical support for performance claims relative to unfair alternatives.

defender_r1_output.pass_1_analysis:
The critique raises several valid concerns about the methodology and experimental design of the proof-of-concept. However, I believe the core approach and intent of the work are sound, and many of the critiques can be addressed through clarification or additional analysis.

F1 - The critic is correct that the choice of baselines is important, as suboptimal or outdated baselines could make the proposed methods appear more competitive than they truly are. The paper should provide more details on the specific baselines used, their theoretical properties, and justification for why they represent the current state-of-the-art in unfair algorithms for these problems.

F2 - The critic raises a good point about the sensitivity of the fair algorithms to accurate prior knowledge of the group probability p. The paper should include ablation experiments to quantify the fairness and utility degradation under p misspecification, as the critic suggests.

F3 - The critique about the limitations of using raw average value as the prophet performance metric is valid. The paper should report normalized competitive ratios in addition to raw averages to provide a more meaningful and comparable measure of algorithm performance.

F4 - The concern about the real-world datasets potentially violating the theoretical assumptions of the online sequential model is reasonable. The paper should provide more details on the dataset characteristics, arrival order generation, and statistical tests for permutation-invariance and group-value independence to address this critique.

rebuttals.justification: The paper will be updated to explicitly list and cite the baseline algorithms used, provide their theoretical performance guarantees in the multi-color setting, and include results from any other relevant unfair state-of-the-art methods. This will address the critic's concern about the baselines potentially being suboptimal or outdated.

The paper will include ablation experiments that perturb the prior probability p (e.g., ±10-20% misspecification) and measure the resulting fairness violation (|P[select G] - true p|) and utility drop. This will demonstrate the sensitivity of the fair algorithms to accurate knowledge of p, as the critic has raised.

The paper will report normalized competitive ratios (E[selected]/E[offline max]) alongside the raw average values for all algorithms and parameter settings. This will address the critic's concern about raw averages being incomparable across different value scales and expected maximum values.

The paper will provide more details on the real-world datasets used, including the arrival order generation process, tests for permutation-invariance (e.g., resampling arrival orders), group-value independence (conditional statistics), and stationarity. This will demonstrate that the real-world data satisfies the key assumptions of the theoretical online sequential model, or quantify any deviations.

defender_r1_output.verdict_rationale: The critic has raised several valid concerns about the experimental methodology, which can be addressed through additional analysis and reporting as outlined in the rebuttals. While the core approach and intent of the work appear sound, there are still some open questions that would benefit from empirical verification, particularly around the sensitivity to prior probability p misspecification (F2) and the validity of the real-world data assumptions (F4). An empirical test is the appropriate next step to fully validate the methodology.

challenges.reasoning: The rebuttal specifically commits to listing/citing baselines, providing their multi-color guarantees, and including other relevant SOTA unfair methods—directly matching the evidence_test and resolving the concern that baselines may not represent true SOTA, making comparisons uninformative.

The rebuttal promises ablation experiments perturbing p by ±10-20% and measuring fairness violation/utility drop—precisely the evidence_test requested, which mechanistically tests and demonstrates robustness to p misspecification or lack of advance knowledge, fully addressing the failure mode.

The rebuttal commits to reporting normalized competitive ratios (E[selected]/E[offline max]) alongside raw averages across all settings—exactly the evidence_test, which resolves incomparability due to varying value scales/E[max] by enabling relative performance assessment.

The rebuttal promises detailed dataset descriptions, arrival order generation, permutation-invariance tests (resampling), conditional independence stats, and stationarity checks—directly implementing the evidence_test to validate or quantify deviations from IID/permutation assumptions, resolving the distribution shift concern.

I will maintain my original positions, as the methodology appears to be sound and the critic's challenges have been satisfactorily resolved.
